body_txt: A character steps out of their normal role, due to anything from being sick to going on vacation to being locked up in prison. The rest of the cast are confident they can handle it, and may even think they can do a better job. Naturally, they fail terribly. The ironic thing is that it's often not that the original person necessarily does a great job, merely that they do an adequate job, or even just that they do it when no one else really wants to. An added source of humor can come from how inconsequential the job seems, and this can tie into An Aesop about minor details being important. Interestingly, this can apply to both villainous and heroic bosses. When an upstart villain tries to replace a Magnificent Bastard in these scenarios, the comeuppance can be spectacular . There is a big overlap between this trope and the "Iron Law of Institutions" coined by Jonathan Schwartz note "The people who control institutions care first and foremost about their power within the institution rather than the power of the institution itself. Thus, they would rather the institution "fail" while they remain in power within the institution than for the institution to "succeed" if that requires them to lose power within the institution." : someone following this trope may go as far as sabotaging the group's overall effectiveness to make it seem like they're needed more than they really are . This can be deadly to a group, especially once something finally removes the "needed" member from power permanently . The oldest television version of this trope usually involved sitcoms and vacationing wives — see A Day in Her Apron — although that particular version is a Discredited Trope , since we've gradually come to realize that how good one is at housework has nothing to do with what's between one's legs. Named for Lord Havelock Vetinari of the Discworld series, the ruler of Ankh-Morpork , who has made himself so utterly indispensable to the city's continued functioning that despite his nature (he's either an Anti-Villain or a very ruthless Anti-Hero , depending on whom you ask), any attempt to remove or replace him is likely to end in disaster. note Indeed, Vetinari deliberately allowed himself to be deposed on two occasions — and nearly on a third — to ensure the populace were reminded of how disastrous the alternative could be. When the time was right, he re-asserted power and restored normality, leaving his opponents utterly discredited. Often obtained with the careful use of Bread and Circuses to appease the common person and undermine support for any critics of the regime. Compare Ultimate Job Security , where someone does a truly bad job and/or is extremely unprofessional but doesn't get fired; George Jetson Job Security , where a character returns mostly for continuity reasons ; Can't Kill You, Still Need You , for more dramatic cases; and Lesser of Two Evils , for broader examples of taking a less-bad option. Contrast The Dilbert Principle , where leadership is intentionally given to in competent group members to keep them from doing more damage elsewhere. Compare The Heart , the glue and morality of a team, and The Reliable One . Contrast Just Fine Without You , in which the person who left hoped for this trope but this trope was averted. Compare with Permanent Elected Official . Compare with Better the Devil You Know , when the current villain is kept around in fear of an even worse replacement. This trope can lead to An Aesop addressing why it's wrong for the Planet of Hats to practice Klingon Scientists Get No Respect . This may be one reason for a Short-Lived Leadership . See also Removing the Crucial Teammate .
